title: Ed Sullivan
subtitle: The Man Who Introduced America to Rock & Roll

scenes.narration: It was Sunday night in America, and for twenty-three remarkable years, that meant one thing.
scenes.visual_intent: It was Sunday night in America, and for twenty-three remarkable years, that meant one thing.

scenes.narration: Joy. Excitement. Something to lift the national spirit. On February 9th, 1964, The Beatles made their American television debut on “The Ed Sullivan Show.” The screams from the studio audience were so loud that the band could barely hear themselves play.

scenes.narration: That mattered. It mattered deeply. Of course, it wasn’t always smooth. There were famous moments of tension. When The Doors appeared in 1967, Sullivan’s producers asked them to change the lyric “girl we couldn’t get much higher” in “Light My Fire.” The band agreed, then defiantly sang the original line anyway.
scenes.visual_intent: That mattered.

scenes.narration: Ed Sullivan ran his show like a gentleman ran his home. He had standards. But he also had the courage to keep inviting the future onto his stage.

scenes.narration: What made Sullivan so effective was that he never pretended to be hip. He was a man of his generation, born at the beginning of the twentieth century, who simply loved excellence and recognized authenticity when he saw it.

scenes.narration: Ed Sullivan passed away in 1974, but his influence never really left us. Every time you see a late-night host introduce a new band, every time a young artist gets their big break on television, you’re seeing the echo of what Sullivan created.
